skill: Evaluating statistical claims: Observational studies and experiments
stem: At the end of a popular radio program, the host asked listeners to call in and answer the question, “Do you support the proposed change to local zoning laws?” The host later announced that of the calls received, 32% answered “Yes” and 65% answered “No.” Which of the following best explains why these results are unlikely to represent the views of the entire city's residents?

answerOptions.content: The percentages do not add up to 100%, so any conclusions are invalid.
The host did not allow listeners enough time to call in.
Those who called in were not a random sample of the city's residents.

rationale: Call-in polls produce self-selected samples that are not random. Only listeners with strong opinions choose to call, so the responses do not reliably represent the broader population. Choice A is wrong because percentages need not sum to 100 if some declined to choose. Choice B is irrelevant. Choice D is a misuse of expected vote splits.
